data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from coordinated slave-revolt and class insurrection exploiting palace dependency on captive labor, while Egypt's reliance on corvée rather than chattel slavery and its ethnic-integration ideology prevented comparable rupture", "mechanism": "Palace economies were uniquely vulnerable because their redistributive systems depended on unfree labor concentrated in palace-controlled textile workshops, agricultural estates, and military units. Widespread debt-bondage and captive populations (documented at Pylos, Ugarit, Hattusa) created structural antagonism. Triggering event: simultaneous liberation of Thracian, Anatolian, and Syrian captives disrupted administrative capacity. Egyptian labor organization relied on seasonal corvée with rotating village obligations and ethnic incorporation (meshwesh, shasu integrated into military), preventing solidarity-based insurrection.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean", "Hittite", "Ugaritic", "Levantine"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Neo-Hittite states", "Philistine pentapolis"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Destruction layers showing evidence of internal burning from lower-town up rather than from citadels or harbors down; absence of elite goods in destruction debris suggesting targeted looting", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B or Ugaritic texts documenting slave uprising, labor flight, or palace counter-insurgency measures immediately pre-destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Comparative evidence that Egyptian New Kingdom labor unrest (e.g. Deir el-Medina strikes under Ramesses III) were systematically suppressed without state collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological demonstration that Mycenaean or Hittite palaces had reduced slave/captive dependency relative to earlier periods", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Revival of 19th-century Marxist-slave-revolt historiography (Childe, Andreev) largely abandoned in current scholarship. Egypt's Deir el-Medina strikes (year 29 Ramesses III) actually demonstrate labor militancy and state fragility, potentially undermining Egyptian exceptionalism claim."}
